channel.title: AGSIST Daily — Morning Agricultural Intelligence Briefing
channel.description: Free daily agricultural market briefing for corn, soybean, and grain producers. Overnight surprises, farmer actions, market analysis — every morning at 5 AM CT.

image.title: AGSIST Daily — Morning Agricultural Intelligence Briefing

item.title: HOGS SURGE SIX PERCENT AS CATTLE CRASH
item.description: Lean hogs exploded 6% to $104.50 while live cattle crashed 2.1% to $243.60, the biggest one-day split between the complexes since March. The fund flow tells the story: managed money is rotating out of cattle futures and into hogs on cutout strength, but cattle's breakdown says the seasonal support just cracked. Corn gained a half-cent to $4.77 on planting delays, but the real story is livestock tearing itself apart. Livestock split violently while grains drift on planting delays. • Hogs Explode Six Percent • Cattle Crash Through Support • Grains Drift Higher Read more: https://agsist.com/daily/2026-05-12

item.title: SILVER EXPLODES SIX PERCENT AS GRAINS DRIFT
item.description: Silver exploded 5.9% to $86.02, the biggest one-day move since March, while corn drifted a half penny lower to $4.73 and beans barely budged at $12.16. The metals are pricing Middle East supply disruption risk that agricultural commodities haven't found yet. With planting week starting and prevent plant deadlines three weeks out, the grain complex is waiting for weather, not geopolitics. Metals lead while corn and beans mark time ahead of planting week. • Grains Mark Time • Cattle Rally Back • Metals Explode Read more: https://agsist.com/daily/2026-05-11

item.title: GRAINS RALLY SETS UP PLANTING WEEK SHOWDOWN
item.description: Corn jumped to $4.71, its best close since March, as soybeans surged 1.5% to $12.08 on Friday's short covering ahead of China trade talks. The grains complex is coiled at the highest levels in eight weeks, but the real test comes this week: prevent-plant deadlines hit across the Belt starting Memorial Day, and 40% of intended soybean acres remain unplanted. Either the weather cooperates or the calendar starts pricing disaster. Corn and beans close at weekly highs as Iran tensions ease, but prevent-plant deadline looms over Memorial Day. • Grains Rally Into Calendar Crunch • Livestock Closes Week Red Read more: https://agsist.com/daily/2026-05-10

item.title: HOGS CRASH NINE PERCENT AS FEEDERS FLIP SCRIPT
item.description: Lean hogs crashed 8.6% Friday to $90.88, the worst single-session drop in six months as the technical floor finally gave way. Live cattle held $248.90, down just a half percent, but feeders flipped positive at $367.38, up three-tenths on the week. The cattle complex is telling two different stories now, and hogs just told theirs loud. Cattle complex splits as feeder momentum turns while lean hogs crater on technical breakdown. • Hogs Crater, Cattle Split • Grains End Week Mixed • Energy Costs Stay Elevated Read more: https://agsist.com/daily/2026-05-09

item.title: CATTLE CRASH THROUGH $250 FLOOR ON FEEDER BREAKDOWN
item.description: Live cattle broke $250.05, down 1.4%, with feeders leading the breakdown at $366.18, off 1.8%. The feeder weakness confirms what Monday's question asked: the support at $250 didn't hold, and the breakdown through key technical levels is real. Boxed beef dropped sharply and the Iran oil premium is evaporating as diplomatic channels open, removing the energy cost tailwind that propped up the complex all week. Both complexes fold as boxed beef drops and Iran tensions ease energy premium. • Cattle Complex Breaks • Grains Mark Time • Energy Premium Evaporates Read more: https://agsist.com/daily/2026-05-08

item.title: CATTLE HOLD $253 AS OIL CRASH EASES INPUT COSTS
item.description: Live cattle held $253.47, up two-tenths, with feeders tracking right alongside at $372.95. The cattle complex is acting like yesterday's call was right: feeder leadership changes everything, breakdown thesis officially dead. Crude's 3% crash to $92.88 takes immediate pressure off diesel and freight costs, giving cattle feeders room to stay patient with their bids. Feeder strength confirms the breakdown thesis is dead while crude's 3% drop cuts diesel pressure. • Cattle Hold Above $252 • Grains Drift Higher on Energy Relief • Energy Costs Crater on Iran Deal Talk Read more: https://agsist.com/daily/2026-05-07

item.title: CATTLE SURGE PAST $252 AS CRUDE CRASHES
item.description: Cattle closed $252.93, up 4% and back above the $252 line that broke last week. But the real story is feeders: $372.23, up 1.4% and finally leading higher instead of dragging the complex down. When the laggard becomes the leader, the breakdown thesis dies. Oil's 6% crash to $94.54 removes the cost squeeze that's been pressuring margins. Feeder strength flips the script while oil's 6% drop eases cost pressures across the board. • Cattle Rally Past $252 • Grains Drift on Profit Taking • Oil Crash Eases Costs Read more: https://agsist.com/daily/2026-05-06

item.title: CATTLE HOLD $251 AS MARKETS MARK TIME
item.description: Live cattle closed $251.95, holding the line one day after breaking Monday's $252 support. The bounce is thin but it's there — feeders dropped harder at $366.98, down 1.4%, suggesting the correction isn't finished but maybe pausing. Yesterday's breakdown call is still live: next real support sits at $245, and today's action doesn't change that math. Live cattle found buyers just above yesterday's breakdown level while grains drifted lower in thin trade. • Cattle Find Thin Support • Grains Drift Quietly Lower Read more: https://agsist.com/daily/2026-05-05

item.title: CATTLE BREAK $252 AS OIL SURGES 3%
item.description: Live cattle closed $253.00, down 98 cents and decisively below the $252 floor that was supposed to hold the breakout story together. The break happened with volume and conviction — not the drift we've been watching, but actual selling. Friday's call on holding pricing discipline above $252 didn't make it through the weekend. The breakout thesis is off the table. Live cattle crashed through Friday's floor while crude oil spiked on supply concerns. • Cattle Break Support → Lock remaining cattle above $250 — next support isn't until $245. • Oil Spike Pressures Costs • Grains Hold Pattern Read more: https://agsist.com/daily/2026-05-04

item.title: WEEK AHEAD: CATTLE TEST $250 FLOOR
item.description: Cattle closed Friday at $253.00, down from Thursday's highs but still holding above the $250 level that's become the market's line in the sand. The livestock selloff that crushed hogs 9% over two days is testing whether cattle's breakout has real conviction or just borrowed momentum from energy's rally. This week's fed cattle trade and Friday's Cattle on Feed report will decide which story the market believes. Friday's livestock selloff sets the stage for a make-or-break week in the cattle complex. • Cattle Floor Test • Grains Coil Tighter • Week Ahead Setup Read more: https://agsist.com/daily/2026-05-03

item.title: HOGS CRASH 9% AS CATTLE BREAKOUT STALLS
item.description: Lean hogs crashed 9.2% to $92.83 Friday, the biggest single-day drop since January, as the weekly cutout report finally caught up with reality. Live cattle gave back ground to $253.00, testing whether last week's breakout above $252 has real conviction or just momentum money looking for an exit. The week that started with cattle hitting 52-week highs ended with livestock selling and nowhere to hide. Energy complex unwound Friday's gains while grains stayed range-bound. • Livestock Correction → Watch $252 in cattle — break closes the breakout story. • Energy Gives Back • Grains Mark Time Read more: https://agsist.com/daily/2026-05-02

item.title: CATTLE FADE AS GRAINS MARK TIME
item.description: Cattle dropped 0.4% to $254.03, the first real test since breaking above $250 on momentum three days ago. The pullback is mild but the funds are watching — managed money added 8,000 contracts this week and needs to see the floor hold. Grains stayed pinned in place with corn at $4.78 and beans flat at $12.03, waiting for next week's planting progress to matter. Live cattle slip from breakout levels while corn and beans hold tight ranges. • Cattle Test Breakout → Hold pricing discipline above $252 — below that level questions the breakout thesis. • Grains Mark Time Read more: https://agsist.com/daily/2026-05-01

item.title: CRUDE CRASHES AS CATTLE HOLD GAINS
item.description: Crude oil crashed $4.81 to $104.22 — its worst single-day drop in three months — but cattle shrugged off the energy selloff and held yesterday's breakout above $255. The divergence says something important: cattle's momentum has fund support that crude's flash rally never earned. Live futures closed at $255.10, down just 70 cents from yesterday's 52-week high. Oil's 4.4% plunge reverses Tuesday's energy rally while livestock extends breakout. • Cattle Hold Breakout → Price remaining summer cattle inventory above $250 — the breakout has legs. • Energy Complex Reverses • Grains Track Lower Read more: https://agsist.com/daily/2026-04-30

item.title: CATTLE HIT 52-WEEK HIGH AS CRUDE EXPLODES
item.description: Live cattle hit a 52-week high at $253.45, up 1.8%, extending Monday's breakout call to an eighth straight session above $245. The funds are backing the move with fresh length as crude oil exploded 4% overnight to $103.34, dragging energy costs higher just as planting season hits the critical May window. Yesterday's cattle breakout call is paying off, but the energy surge changes the input cost equation for producers racing against calendar pressure. Live cattle reached $253.45 while crude oil surged 4% overnight on Middle East tensions. • Cattle Extend Breakout → Hold remaining fed cattle inventory — momentum is accelerating with fund support. • Crude Oil Explodes Higher • Grains Mark Time Read more: https://agsist.com/daily/2026-04-29

item.title: CATTLE BREAK THROUGH AS CRUDE SURGES
item.description: Live cattle exploded to $248.97, up 1.7% and the third consecutive close above $245 — yesterday's pricing trigger just delivered. The funds added 8,000 contracts overnight as feedlots finally capitulated to summer demand that's been building since March. Crude's 3% surge to $100.54 is dragging corn along for the ride, but the real story is cattle momentum that's now undeniable. Live futures hit $248.97, triggering yesterday's pricing call while WTI jumps 3%. • Cattle Breakout Confirmed → Price remaining summer cattle — the breakout above $245 for three sessions confirms the uptrend. • Crude Drags Corn Higher • Planting Window Tightens Read more: https://agsist.com/daily/2026-04-28

item.title: GRAINS FLATLINE AS PLANTING WINDOW TIGHTENS
item.description: Corn barely moved to $4.67, up just 1.5 cents, as traders wait for weather drama that hasn't arrived yet. Soybeans sat virtually unchanged at $11.80 while cattle futures extended last week's surge, with live cattle adding another 0.7% to $245.22. The grain complex is marking time in narrow ranges, but planting pressure builds with each passing day. Corn and beans drift sideways while cattle hold recent gains and energy weakens. • Grains Mark Time • Cattle Momentum Holds → Price summer cattle if live futures close above $245 for three consecutive sessions. • Energy Retreat Read more: https://agsist.com/daily/2026-04-27

item.title: MIXED SIGNALS AHEAD OF CRITICAL PLANTING WEEK
item.description: Commodity markets enter a pivotal week with mixed signals across the board. Corn held flat at $4.63 as of Friday's close while December contracts edged higher to $4.84, suggesting cautious optimism despite planting pressure. With optimal planting windows narrowing and weather forecasts becoming the primary driver, producers face tough decisions on pricing and risk management. Grains steady, cattle strong, energy weak as weather takes command. • Week Ahead Setup • Cattle Momentum Continues → Price remaining fed cattle if live futures hold above $245 through Wednesday's close. • Energy Divergence Read more: https://agsist.com/daily/2026-04-26

item.title: PLANTING PRESSURE BUILDS AS GRAINS SLIDE
item.description: Spring planting pressure finally showed in grain futures as the week closed, with nearby corn sliding to $4.55 and Chicago wheat dropping to $6.08. Live cattle bucked the trend, rallying 2% to $248.30 on strong demand fundamentals. The week showcased the classic April tension: planting season price weakness meets seasonal demand strength. Corn and wheat fell sharply Friday while cattle surged to 52-week highs. • WEEK IN REVIEW • CATTLE SURGE CONTINUES → Cattle feeders should price summer cattle if futures hold above $245 when markets open Monday. • WHAT TO WATCH MONDAY Read more: https://agsist.com/daily/2026-04-25

item.title: MILK CRASHES 6% AS GRAINS INCH HIGHER
item.description: Class III milk crashed 6.4% overnight to $16.86, the sharpest drop in months as dairy margins compress heading into summer. Corn managed a modest gain to $4.65 while soybeans edged to $11.75, both marking time as planting season enters its critical phase. Class III tumbled overnight while corn crept to $4.65 amid quiet trade. • Grains Mark Time • Dairy Disaster → Dairy producers should hedge remaining 2026 production if Class III bounces above $17.50 next week. • Energy Softens Read more: https://agsist.com/daily/2026-04-24

item.title: NATURAL GAS SPIKES AS GRAINS HOLD STEADY
item.description: Grain futures drifted in narrow ranges Thursday as corn reached $4.64 per bushel and December corn climbed to $4.84. Natural gas provided the day's fireworks with a 5.5% surge to $2.87, breaking a string of quiet energy sessions. Planting pressure kept crop futures anchored despite decent demand underneath. Energy volatility returns while crop futures mark time during planting season. • Grains Mark Time • Livestock Pressure • Energy Volatility Returns → Lock remaining propane and diesel if natural gas holds above $2.75 through Friday close. Read more: https://agsist.com/daily/2026-04-23

item.title: GRAINS DRIFT AS PLANTING PRESSURE MOUNTS
item.description: Corn futures crept higher with nearby contracts at $4.64, up a penny on the session. Soybeans softened modestly to $11.96 as planting season momentum builds across the Corn Belt. The grain complex showed little conviction as producers focus shifts from price action to field conditions. Corn holds narrow gains while beans ease slightly amid steady weather conditions. • Grains Mark Time • Livestock Mixed Signals • Energy Firms Modestly Read more: https://agsist.com/daily/2026-04-22

item.title: CORN HOLDS GAINS AS BEANS EDGE HIGHER
item.description: Corn held steady at $4.59 after Monday's weather-driven rally, while soybeans added 2.75 cents to $11.87 on continued South American tightness. Livestock struggled with feeder cattle dropping 1.1% as producers face margin pressure. Markets consolidate after Monday's planting scare as weather watch intensifies. • Grains Hold Steady • Livestock Divergence • Energy Steady Read more: https://agsist.com/daily/2026-04-21

item.title: CORN JUMPS ON PLANTING CONCERNS
item.description: Corn nearby futures jumped to $4.58, up 8.5 cents in a move that caught traders off guard as planting window pressure builds. The rally came as crude oil spiked 5.7% to $87.26, adding input cost concerns to weather worries. December corn held steady at $4.78, suggesting the market sees this as a short-term squeeze rather than a fundamental shift. Nearby corn rallied sharply while energy spiked, but livestock struggled across the board. • Grains Split Story → Price 25% of remaining old crop corn if nearby futures hold above $4.55 through Wednesday close. • Livestock Pressure • Energy Shock → Lock remaining diesel needs if WTI breaks above $88 on Tuesday morning. Read more: https://agsist.com/daily/2026-04-20

item.title: MIXED WEEK SETS STAGE FOR WEATHER WATCH
item.description: Grains ended the week with mixed signals as corn eased to $4.49 while soybeans gained to $11.83 on Friday's close. The divergence reflects different planting timelines and weather sensitivities heading into a critical week for field conditions. Livestock struggled across the board with live cattle at $247.35 and hogs sliding to $101.05. Corn dipped to $4.49 as planting accelerated while beans gained ground ahead of Monday's open. • Week Ahead Weather Focus • Livestock Pressure Builds → Cattle producers should lock protection if live futures break below $245 Monday morning. • Energy Story Shifts Read more: https://agsist.com/daily/2026-04-19

item.title: GRAINS CAP CHOPPY WEEK AS PLANTING ACCELERATES
item.description: A turbulent week in ag markets wrapped up with grain futures pulling back as spring planting gained momentum across the Corn Belt. Corn closed at $4.49 per bushel, down 10 cents Friday, while Chicago wheat dropped to $5.91. The week's bigger story played out in livestock, where cattle strength collided with an 8% hog collapse that left lean hogs at $93.40. Corn and wheat finished lower while livestock diverged sharply in volatile Friday trading. • Week In Review • Livestock Divide → Cattle producers should lock protection if live futures break below $245 on Monday. Hog producers consider aggressive forward pricing if June futures rally above $95. • Energy Pressure Read more: https://agsist.com/daily/2026-04-18

item.title: GRAINS DRIFT LOWER AS PLANTING PICKS UP
item.description: Corn futures eased fractionally to $4.58 nearby while soybeans slipped to $11.59 as early planting progress removes some weather premium. The action remains muted as farmers balance between field work and pricing decisions, though oats bucked the trend with a solid 1.7% gain to $3.48 on persistent quality concerns from last year's challenging harvest. Small declines across corn and soybeans while oats rally 1.7% on quality concerns. • Grains Ease Gently → Use any weather scares to price remaining old crop aggressively — these steady levels won't last if we hit extended wet weather. • Cattle Retreat → Cattle producers should view any significant weakness as protection-buying opportunities — margins remain historically strong. • Energy Mixed Story → Lock remaining diesel needs while prices soften — planting season demand typically reverses crude weakness quickly. Read more: https://agsist.com/daily/2026-04-17

item.title: MILK CRASHES 5% AS GRAINS STAY STEADY
item.description: Class III milk crashed to $17.34 overnight, down a brutal 5.1% in a stark reminder of dairy's volatility problem we discussed yesterday. Meanwhile, corn barely budged at $4.51 per bushel and soybeans held $11.65 as planting season begins in earnest across the Corn Belt. Dairy producers face reality check while corn and beans hold pattern through early planting. • Grains Hold Steady → Keep weather radars open and pricing fingers ready — any significant rain threats could spark quick rallies to move remaining old crop. • Dairy Reality Check → Dairy producers should reassess risk management strategies immediately — this volatility demands active hedging, not hope. • Energy Inputs Slip → Lock remaining diesel needs this week — planting season demand concentration can override broader crude weakness. Read more: https://agsist.com/daily/2026-04-16

item.title: GRAINS INCH HIGHER AS PLANTING BEGINS
item.description: Corn nearby futures gained 1.25 cents to $4.46/bu as producers begin planting across the Corn Belt, with soybeans adding 1.75 cents to $11.63/bu. Both markets remain in cautious mode as every field day becomes precious, while Class III milk crashed 5.2% overnight on renewed oversupply concerns. Corn and soybeans gain modestly while dairy crashes 5% overnight. • Grains & Oilseeds → Price remaining old crop aggressively on any weather scares — planting delays could spark sharp rallies in already tight supplies. • Livestock & Dairy → Lock remaining cattle protection immediately — margins this strong don't last. Dairy producers should reassess expansion plans after this overnight reality check. • Energy & Inputs → Lock remaining diesel needs this week — planting season typically drives prices higher as regional demand concentrates. Read more: https://agsist.com/daily/2026-04-15

item.title: GRAINS DRIFT AS PLANTING WINDOW OPENS
item.description: Grain markets treaded water Tuesday as planting season officially arrives, with corn futures barely budging at $4.42/bu while soybeans gained a modest 3.5 cents to $11.66/bu. The action reflects a market caught between seasonal planting pressure and uncertainty about weather patterns that will define the next 30 days. Corn holds near $4.42 while soybeans edge higher ahead of critical weather week. • Grains & Oilseeds → Monitor 7-day forecasts closely — any significant rain threats could spark quick rallies to price remaining old crop. • Livestock & Dairy → Cattle producers should use any strength to extend protection — these margin levels rarely sustain through volatile periods. • Energy & Inputs → Lock remaining diesel needs this week — planting demand surge typically moves prices higher through May. Read more: https://agsist.com/daily/2026-04-14

item.title: CORN HOLDS STEADY AS PLANTING PRESSURE BUILDS
item.description: Corn held flat at $4.44/bu as producers gear up for what could be a make-or-break planting week across the Corn Belt. Soybeans eased slightly to $11.74/bu on profit-taking, while wheat gained ground at $5.83/bu. With optimal planting windows opening, weather will increasingly drive price action over fundamentals. Grains mixed in quiet Monday session while cattle maintain historic strength. • Grains & Oilseeds → Monitor 7-day forecasts obsessively — any wet weather threats could spark quick rallies to price remaining bushels. • Livestock & Dairy → Lock remaining cattle exposure immediately — margins this extreme have short shelf lives in volatile markets. • Energy & Inputs → Secure remaining diesel contracts this week — planting season demand spikes can move prices fast. Read more: https://agsist.com/daily/2026-04-13
